nesa_terms.2: Identify Define Describe Explain Analyse Justify Evaluate
nesa_terms.3: Identify: Recognise and name. Define: State meaning and identify essential qualities. Describe: Provide characteristics and features. Explain: Relate cause and effect; make relationships between things evident; provide why and/or how. Analyse: Identify components and the relationship between them; draw out and relate implications. Justify: Support an argument or conclusion. Evaluate: Make a judgement based on criteria; determine the value of.
nesa_terms.4: When you identify a problem, you recognise and name its core components. To define a concept, you state its meaning while identifying the essential qualities that distinguish it from other concepts. When you describe a system, you provide its characteristics and observable features. To explain something, you relate cause and effect and make the relationships between different elements evident. When you analyse code or design, you identify its components and the relationships between them. To justify a decision, you support your argument with evidence and reasoning. When you evaluate a solution, you make judgements based on established criteria and determine its value.
nesa_terms.5: Software engineering professionals regularly use NESA key terms to communicate about their work and reasoning. Identification is the first critical step in problem-solving, requiring developers to recognise and accurately name the specific issues and requirements they face. Definition establishes shared understanding by stating the precise meaning of technical concepts while identifying the essential qualities that distinguish them. Description provides stakeholders with comprehensive characteristics and features of systems, enabling informed decision-making. Explanation demonstrates deep understanding by relating cause and effect and making explicit the relationships between system components that might otherwise remain implicit. Analysis is the technical skill of decomposing complex systems into their constituent parts and understanding their interrelationships. Justification of design decisions using evidence strengthens professional credibility and enables knowledge transfer within teams. Evaluation with established criteria ensures that solutions meet requirements and perform optimally. Mastery of these terms enables Year 12 students to transition confidently into professional software engineering discourse and practice.
